turns.content: Hey, I've read about the macro crates like proc-macro and built-in macros, but I still don't get why we use things like todo!, warn!, or unreachable!. Is it just for documentation?

Not quite, those aren't just for docs. They are compile-time checks that catch errors before your code ever runs on the target platform. The macro crates like proc-macro and built-in macros let you write these safety markers directly in your Rust source files without needing extra dependencies.
But wait, if it's compile-time, why do I see warnings at runtime? Aren't those supposed to be caught by the compiler?

Exactly, that is a common point of confusion. The macro crates like proc-macro and built-in macros generate code at compile time so you can use cfg-gated placeholders to mark incomplete branches safely. When you leave those markers in place without the necessary condition flags, they trigger warnings or panics at runtime instead of failing silently.

Imagine I'm porting a legacy C project into Rust, where there are many temporary functions that are still being written. How should I mark these incomplete branches so the compiler knows they aren't supposed to run at all?

For those temporary functions you're marking as incomplete, you can use the cfg-gated placeholder macro called todo! which tells the Rust compiler this code is intentionally left out and will panic at runtime if executed. This differs from unreachable! because unreachable! causes a compile-time failure rather than a runtime one, so it's used when you want to guarantee the code never runs at all. Since your legacy C project likely has these incomplete functions scattered throughout, using cfg-gated placeholders with todo! allows them to be compiled without errors while still serving as clear markers that they are not meant to be executed in production.

That makes sense for runtime panics, but what if I want the compiler to warn me about this code path at compile time instead of letting it run? Can I use a different macro or trait bound to catch these incomplete branches earlier?

You can't use a single macro to replace todo! because that's specifically designed for runtime panics, but you can combine macros with attributes to achieve what you want. Instead of just using the plain macro, you would wrap it in a #[allow(dead_code)] or #[cfg(feature = "dev")] attribute so it only compiles during development phases and gets removed from release builds. This way, the compiler won't even try to execute it at runtime because the cfg-gated conditional prevents compilation of that code path entirely when the feature flag is off.

Wait, I thought macros like this were always compiled into the final binary? If I use #[cfg(feature = "dev")] then shouldn't they still be part of the release binary since it's just a build-time check?

No, you are mistaken about how cfg-gated code works because Rust does not include any code that is gated behind false conditions in the final artifact. When you compile with feature flags disabled, the compiler simply removes those entire blocks from the output entirely rather than keeping them as dead code or warnings. This means your incomplete branches marked with cfg-gated placeholders will disappear completely from the release binary, ensuring no one ever accidentally executes them at runtime.

Imagine I have a feature flag that might be missing in production. If I write standard code for an incomplete branch, the program runs but warns at runtime. But if I want to catch this immediately without running anything, what tool should I use instead of just writing normal if statements?

You should use the macro called todo! for incomplete branches. Unlike regular conditionals that execute at runtime when the code runs, the macro macro! will fail compilation immediately because it is designed to catch errors before your program ever starts executing. This prevents runtime failures by ensuring the compiler rejects code containing unimplemented logic rather than letting it pass and crash later.

That sounds good for catching bugs early, but what if I just want to remind myself to implement something without stopping compilation? Is there a different macro that allows the code to compile while warning me?

Yes, you can use the warn! macro which is similar to println! but prints a warning message instead of outputting data. This lets your code compile successfully so you can continue working, yet it will print a warning at runtime when that branch executes if the feature flag is enabled.

I'm trying to understand how Rust macros handle incomplete code blocks, but I keep thinking they just throw runtime errors like Python exceptions. Is that correct? I've been using try-catch patterns in JavaScript so far.

That's a common misunderstanding when coming from dynamic languages. In Rust, the macros you mentioned don't execute at runtime to cause failures; they are evaluated entirely by the compiler during compilation. The macro `todo!` will fail with a compile-time error if used incorrectly or if it is ever actually executed, but its primary purpose is to help developers mark branches as incomplete before writing the real implementation. It does not behave like Python exceptions because Rust enforces safety at compile time rather than catching errors later.

Oh, so I shouldn't use try-catch logic for macros then? But I noticed some code uses cfg-gated placeholders and I thought those were just runtime flags to skip parts of the program. Can you clarify what `cfg` actually does here compared to a standard if statement?

You're right about not needing try-catch patterns; Rust's type system handles most safety checks before the code even runs. The `cfg` attribute is different from an `if` statement because it allows conditional compilation based on build flags, target architectures, or feature gates defined in Cargo.toml. When you use a macro like `unreachable!`, the compiler will generate an error if that line is ever executed at runtime, ensuring that dead code paths are never actually run. This is more strict than just using a regular `if` because it guarantees the branch was intended to be removed permanently.

Got it, so `todo!` and `unreachable!` act as compile-time sentinels rather than runtime checks? I think I get how they prevent accidental execution of incomplete logic without needing extra error handling code. Thanks for clarifying the distinction between cfg-gated sections and normal branches.

Exactly, they serve as sentinel markers that guide the compiler to reject incomplete or unreachable paths before any runtime execution occurs. This approach is particularly useful in large libraries where you want to document TODOs without introducing runtime overhead or unexpected failures. The macros integrate seamlessly with the Rust type system and standard library traits like `std::fmt::Debug` so they can be inspected easily during development cycles.
Perfect, that makes sense for our current project structure. I'll update my documentation to reflect these compile-time markers instead of relying on runtime checks.
That's a great step forward—using these macros will significantly reduce the risk of subtle bugs slipping into production code.
